title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The PR delivers two new endpoints but has several blocking issues: the in-memory refund store is a module-level singleton (causes cross-test pollution and makes `RefundsService` un-instantiable as a true dependency), the history sort is silently broken because it compares `a.created_at` against an `undefined` property (the domain field is `createdAt`), there is no accumulated-refund guard so the same transaction can be refunded in full multiple times, and the DI wiring in `createApp` attaches `RefundsService` to a freshly-created repo instead of the one the injected `PaymentsService` owns. Additionally: three new files use bare `console.log` while the codebase has a structured pino logger with redaction, the history response emits `card_number` alongside `card_last4` (PII field name that will cause confusion and is trivially exploitable if the Transaction type ever gains that property), a missing transaction returns `200 {}` instead of `404`, the history response uses snake_case where the rest of the API uses camelCase, the tests carry almost no real assertions, and the dollar-to-cents conversion heuristic is unsafe and should be replaced by strict input validation.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore leaks state across instances and tests
findings.message: `refundsStore` is declared at module scope, making it a process-wide singleton. Every `RefundsService` instance — including those created by different `createApp()` calls in the test suite — shares the same store. Refunds created in one test pollute every subsequent test, and test order can flip pass/fail outcomes. Move the store inside the class as a private instance field: `private readonly refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {};`

findings.title: Sort comparator uses `created_at` but Transaction field is `createdAt`
findings.message: The comparator accesses `a.created_at` / `b.created_at`, which are both `undefined` on every `Transaction` (the field is `createdAt` per `domain/transaction.ts`). The cast to `any` silences TypeScript but the sort is a no-op — transactions are never ordered newest-first. Fix: `mine.sort((a, b) => b.createdAt.localeCompare(a.createdAt))`.

findings.title: No accumulated-refund guard — same transaction can be fully refunded many times
findings.message: The guard on line 37 only checks that a single incoming `refundAmount` does not exceed `tx.amount`. It does not sum previously-issued refunds. An attacker (or bug) can POST /refunds for the same `transaction_id` repeatedly and receive a full-amount refund each time. Before creating the refund, call `listRefundsForTransaction(transactionId)`, sum the existing amounts, and reject (400/422) if `existingTotal + refundAmount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: RefundsService is wired to a repo that the injected PaymentsService never writes to
findings.message: When `deps.payments` is supplied (e.g. in integration tests that inject a stub), `transactionRepo` is a brand-new empty instance that the injected service does not know about. Any charge made through the injected service is invisible to `RefundsService`, so every refund attempt returns `null`. `AppDeps` should accept an optional `TransactionRepository` so callers can thread the same instance through, or the DI boundary should be lifted so both services always share the repo that was actually used to record charges.

findings.title: `card_number` in API response — PII field name that risks full-PAN exposure
findings.message: `Transaction` has no `card_number` property, so the cast always resolves to `undefined` and falls back to `cardLast4`. The result is that the response already contains the duplicated and confusingly named field `card_number` (value: last 4 digits) alongside `card_last4`. If `Transaction` or a gateway decorator ever gains a `card_number` field, this line silently exposes the full PAN. Remove the `card_number` field entirely; `card_last4` is sufficient and already present.

findings.title: Returns 200 with empty body when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: Returning `200 {}` for a non-existent `transaction_id` is misleading: callers cannot distinguish 'refund created' from 'transaction not found'. The pattern used elsewhere (`notFound()` from `errors.ts`) throws an `AppError` with status 404 which the existing `errorHandler` formats consistently. Replace the `res.status(200).json({})` branch with `next(notFound('Transaction not found'))` (and import `notFound` from `../../errors`).

findings.title: `console.log` used instead of the codebase's structured pino logger
findings.message: `src/logger.ts` exports a pino `Logger` instance with redaction rules for card numbers and auth headers. All existing service code (`paymentsService.ts`) and the error/request-logging middleware use it. The three new `console.log` calls in `refundsService.ts` (lines 25, 55) and the one in `customerHistoryController.ts` (line 15) bypass structured logging, redaction, and the log-level control that silences output in tests. Replace with `import { logger } from '../logger'` and use `logger.info(...)` / `logger.warn(...)` with a structured object.

findings.title: History response uses snake_case — inconsistent with the rest of the API
findings.message: The existing `/charge` and `/transactions/:id` endpoints return camelCase fields (`cardLast4`, `customerId`, `gatewayReference`, `createdAt`). The history endpoint emits snake_case for all the same concepts (`card_last4`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at`). Clients relying on a uniform casing convention will break. Align with the existing convention: use camelCase throughout, or define a canonical response type and serialise it consistently.

findings.title: Dollar-to-cents heuristic silently corrupts refund amounts
findings.message: The comment says 'new clients sometimes send dollars, so handle both', but the detection logic is unreliable. Example: charge of 1000 cents, refund request of `amount: 20` (meaning $20 / 2000 cents). Because 20 < 1000 the branch is never entered, so a 20-cent refund is silently issued instead of rejecting or converting. The correct fix is to drop the heuristic entirely, document that `amount` is always in the smallest currency unit (matching `chargeSchema`'s `z.number().int().positive()`) and return a `400` if the value doesn't meet that contract.

findings.title: Tests carry almost no real assertions and would miss most regressions
findings.message: Three concrete problems: (1) The 'creates a refund' test never asserts `refund.status === 201` or that `refund.body.refund_id`, `amount`, `status` are present — it only checks `toBeDefined()` on the body object, which is always truthy. (2) 'handles missing transactions' uses `toBeLessThan(500)` — a 200 empty-body response (the current bug) passes this. It should be `toBe(404)`. (3) 'returns a list' would pass even if the endpoint 500s, since `res.body` on a supertest error response is still a defined object. Add assertions on status codes, response shapes, sort order, partial-refund amounts, and double-refund rejection.

findings.title: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but never imported anywhere
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` was presumably intended to validate the `/customers/:id/transactions` route's path parameter but is never imported by the history controller or routes. Either wire it up (validate `req.params.id` is a non-empty string) or remove the dead export.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` used to compare `customerId`
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` uses loose equality. `customerId` is `string | undefined`; loose `==` also matches `null`, which can mask data issues. Use `===` to match the strict-equality convention used everywhere else in the codebase.

findings.title: `amount` in refundSchema has no positivity or integer constraint
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` allows zero, negative values, and non-integer floats. The charge schema uses `z.number().int().positive()`. Align: `amount: z.number().int().positive().optional()` to reject nonsensical inputs early rather than letting them propagate to the service layer.
